mappings.rationaleAI: Medieval universities: trivium/quadrivium, founding of universities, humanities education.
Neoplatonism: Academy tradition, Christianization of pagan culture, Islamic scholarship.
Humanism from Cicero through Renaissance: definition of humanities.
School of Athens: Renaissance humanism synthesizing classical and Christian.
Socratic self-examination: humanities vs sciences as paths to knowledge.
Scientist: separation of science from philosophy.
Written World: Hellenistic literary scholarship, philology.
Paganism in Renaissance: classical revival, Church/pagan reconciliation.
Scepticism: Socrates through Descartes, search for universal method.
Rhetoric: Sophists, Roman orators, conflict of rhetoric/philosophy/science.
Roger Bacon: Scholastic method, faith/reason, science/philosophy separation.
Aquinas: Scholastic method, faith/reason, Christianization of Aristotle.
Abbasid caliphate: Islamic humanistic scholarship.
Al-Ghazali: Islamic humanistic tradition.
Byzantium: preservation of Greek manuscripts enabling Renaissance.
Empiricism: universal method, science/philosophy separation.
Greyfriars/Blackfriars: university curricula, Scholastic method.
History's relevance: humanities definition, knowledge explosion.
Mendelssohn: Jewish humanistic scholarship.
Muslim Spain: Islamic humanistic scholarship.

mappings.episodeTitle: Giorgio Vasari's Lives of the Artists
mappings.rationaleAI: Vasari: Renaissance ideal, growth of humanistic scholarship.

mappings.episodeTitle: Goethe and the Science of the Enlightenment
mappings.rationaleAI: Goethe: unified knowledge, ancients vs moderns, humanities vs sciences.
Pliny: Aristotelian knowledge organization, Hellenistic scholarship.
Library of Alexandria: Hellenistic scholarship center.
Translation Movement: Islamic humanistic scholarship, classical preservation.
Epic: Homeric paideia, literary criticism.
Politeness: belles lettres transition, ancients vs moderns.

Sassoon's anti-war poetry, his mentoring of Wilfred Owen, and his diaries as primary sources for 20th-century literary and social history connect to humanistic scholarship and the study of literature as a humanistic discipline.
Munch's art and its critical reception, from his proto-expressionist approach through to his influence on Andy Warhol, connect to humanistic scholarship on visual art and aesthetic movements.

The sublime as an aesthetic category developed through humanistic scholarship from Longinus through 18th-century British thinkers, representing a major thread in the history of art criticism and aesthetic theory.

Problems of attribution, determining the authentic canon, and establishing correct texts from quartos and folios connect to humanistic scholarship including textual criticism and philological methods.

Yeats's poetry, including Easter 1916 with its refrain about terrible beauty, represents a major achievement in humanistic literary scholarship and the study of how literature engages with political reality.

Modernist literary utopias as a chapter in the development of humane letters from the Renaissance to the present (A.3.a), and the quarrel between progressive idealism and anti-utopianism relates to the quarrel between ancients and moderns (A.3.e).
Pope's Essay on Man and The Dunciad engage directly with the quarrel between ancients and moderns (A.3.e) and the growth of modern humanistic scholarship through literary criticism (A.3.f).
The Royal Society's founding exemplifies the separation of science from philosophy and the rise of empirical science (A.3.d).
Burckhardt's narrative of the Renaissance as rebirth of classical learning directly addresses the idea of Renaissance as the ideal of the classical (A.3.a) and Humanism and the new learning (A.3.b).

The intersection of psychoanalysis and literature as humanistic disciplines, and the question of whether psychoanalysis is better understood as literary rather than clinical enterprise, relates to the humanities and humanistic scholarship (A).

The unicorn as a cultural symbol spanning classical, medieval, and early modern periods illustrates how humanistic scholarship traces mythological figures across literary, artistic, and religious traditions (A)

Herodotus's account of Thermopylae as a defining clash between East and West, and the ongoing debate about the battle's significance for democratic governance, represent foundational humanistic scholarship (A)

Uncle Tom's Cabin demonstrates literature's power to shape political action while embedding problematic cultural images; this dual legacy exemplifies the humanities' engagement with social and moral questions (A)

Vase-mania's connection of classical learning, good taste, and material culture into a coherent ideal of refined sensibility exemplifies humanistic scholarship linking archaeological discovery to aesthetic theory (A)
